title: Modules and Packages
summary: Organize code across multiple files using import and export statements and understand module resolution

sections.title: Modules Are Like Books in a Library
sections.content: A single TypeScript file is like a chapter in a book. Modules let you split your program across multiple files, each responsible for one aspect of functionality — exactly like a library where each book covers a different topic. The 'export' keyword makes a function, class, or variable available to other files. The 'import' keyword pulls it in where needed. This organization prevents the chaos of a 5000-line file and lets you reuse code across projects. TypeScript supports ES modules (import/export) natively, which is the modern standard. Each module has its own scope — variables declared in one module do not leak into another unless explicitly exported.

sections.title: Exporting and Importing Utilities Across Files
sections.content: Shows a math utilities module being exported and imported in a main file

sections.title: Named Exports vs Default Exports and Barrel Files
sections.content: Named exports (using 'export' before each declaration) are explicit and let you export multiple items from one module. Default exports ('export default') are for the main feature of a module — each module can have only one. Named exports are preferred in larger codebases because they make imports predictable and enable IDE autocompletion. A common pattern is the 'barrel file' — an index.ts that re-exports everything from a directory, so consumers import from a single path. This is like having a directory index at the front of a filing cabinet telling you which folder contains what.

sections.title: Importing from the Wrong Path or Missing File Extension
sections.content: The #1 intermediate mistake is import path errors — wrong relative path or missing file extension. Wrong approach: 'import { foo } from "./utils/math"' expecting TypeScript to find 'math.ts' without the extension. In Node.js ESM, you must include the extension: './utils/math.js' (even though the source is .ts). Wrong approach: './utils/helper' when the file is actually at './utils/helpers'. Correct: double-check the file exists at the specified path and use .js extension for ESM. TypeScript's path mapping ('paths' in tsconfig.json) can simplify this for shared code.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Refactor a monolithic file into modules. You have a shopping cart system with functions: addItem, removeItem, calculateTotal, and a constant SHIPPING_COST. Split these into three modules: 'cart/cart.ts' (addItem, removeItem), 'cart/pricing.ts' (calculateTotal, SHIPPING_COST), and 'cart/index.ts' (barrel that re-exports everything). Then write a main.ts that imports from './cart/index.js' and uses all functions.
hints: Which functions belong together — what do addItem and removeItem have in common?
How do you re-export something from a barrel file?
What's the difference between 'export' and 'export default' when you have multiple exports?
